steps.title: Restate the hypothesis
steps.content: Ayalew and colleagues hypothesized that the plants' response to kanamycin involves altering their uptake of metals such as iron and zinc. Supporting data must show a difference in metal content between the kanamycin-exposed plants and the control plants.

steps.title: Read what the graph shows
steps.content: The graph plots iron and zinc concentrations for the two groups. The kanamycin-exposed plants have noticeably lower iron and lower zinc than the control plants, consistent with kanamycin altering (specifically reducing) the plants' uptake of both metals.

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'control plants contained higher levels of zinc than iron, but plants exposed to kanamycin contained higher levels of iron than zinc': this flip of relative levels does not match the graph, which shows iron higher than zinc in both groups (misread of the data). ✗
  • Choice B — 'Both groups...more than 200 parts per million of both iron and zinc': even if true, a shared baseline does not show any difference between groups, so it cannot support an 'altering' claim (misread of what would count as support). ✗
  • Choice C — 'Zinc levels were around 300 parts per million in the control plants but nearly 400...in the plants exposed to kanamycin': reverses the direction of the zinc difference shown in the graph and ignores iron (misread of the data). ✗
  • Choice D — 'plants exposed to kanamycin showed lower levels of iron and zinc than the control plants': matches the graph and directly shows kanamycin altering the plants' uptake of both metals named in the hypothesis. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
